primary_diagnosis.name: Suspected Short QT syndrome presenting with unheralded arrhythmic syncope (palpitation-preceded) on a background of familial young sudden cardiac death and early-onset atrial fibrillation

pathophysiology_summary: Short QT syndrome (SQTS) is a rare inherited cardiac channelopathy caused by gain-of-function mutations in cardiac potassium channels (e.g. KCNH2, KCNQ1, KCNJ2) or, less commonly, loss-of-function in L-type calcium channel subunits, producing accelerated ventricular repolarisation and an abnormally short corrected QT interval (QTc, classically <340-360 ms). The shortened and heterogeneous refractory periods create a substrate for re-entry, predisposing to both atrial fibrillation (often at a strikingly young age) and life-threatening polymorphic ventricular tachycardia/ventricular fibrillation, hence syncope, cardiac arrest and sudden death. The heart is structurally normal - SQTS is an electrical, not structural, disorder - so a normal resting examination does not exclude it; diagnosis requires a 12-lead ECG to measure the (abnormally short) QTc, continuous monitoring, and specialist electrophysiology/genetic assessment. In this patient, an unheralded syncope at rest preceded by a brief violent palpitation, the six-month history of unprovoked palpitations, a brother's cardiac arrest at 28, and early-onset atrial fibrillation in the mother together form a red-flag cluster for a malignant inherited channelopathy; the co-occurrence of young sudden death with early-onset AF is a recognised pointer to SQTS specifically. The immediate danger is a recurrent, potentially fatal ventricular arrhythmia. None of the required workup (12-lead ECG/QTc, telemetry, echocardiography, genetics) can be performed over telehealth, so the correct action is immediate emergency assessment with continuous cardiac monitoring while the patient rests quietly; definitive therapy is specialist-directed and typically involves an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD), with quinidine as an antiarrhythmic adjunct in selected cases - decisions that cannot be made remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Inherited arrhythmic syncope (channelopathy - e.g. Short QT syndrome)
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden collapse at rest preceded by a violent palpitation, in a young otherwise-healthy adult, is a red-flag pattern for an arrhythmic (channelopathy) syncope rather than a simple faint.
Common cause of syncope, but occurrence while seated at rest with no prodrome and a preceding palpitation is atypical and more concerning for an arrhythmia.
Loss of consciousness raises seizure, but rapid recovery without tongue-biting, incontinence or a post-ictal state favours arrhythmic (convulsive) syncope.
Considered for arrhythmic syncope with a family history, but a completely normal cardiac examination argues against significant structural disease.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor should treat unheralded syncope-at-rest with a preceding palpitation and a young sudden-death family history as a possible malignant inherited arrhythmia, not a simple faint or seizure.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected Short QT syndrome - unheralded arrhythmic syncope with familial young sudden death and early-onset atrial fibrillation
differential.evidence_basis: Palpitation-preceded syncope at rest (DI-001/DI-002) + brother's cardiac arrest at 28 (DI-003) + early-onset maternal atrial fibrillation (DI-004) + six-month palpitation history (DI-005), with a structurally normal heart, strongly indicate an inherited channelopathy; young SCD plus early-onset AF is a specific pointer to Short QT syndrome.
No prodrome, occurrence at rest, a preceding palpitation and a malignant family history are not vasovagal.
Rapid recovery without tongue-biting or a post-ictal state, with a clear cardiac prodrome (palpitation), argues against primary seizure.
Normal cardiac examination, normal pulses and no murmur/heave argue against significant structural disease (though echo confirms).

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the leading concern is an inherited channelopathy (Short QT syndrome); urgent ECG/monitoring and specialist assessment are mandatory and a benign faint is excluded.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected Short QT syndrome pending 12-lead ECG (an abnormally short QTc) and specialist/genetic assessment
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Cannot be confirmed without a 12-lead ECG (short QTc), continuous monitoring and specialist assessment; the correct action is immediate emergency assessment with monitoring while the patient rests.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is a suspected inherited arrhythmia (Short QT syndrome) requiring an immediate ambulance, ECG and continuous monitoring - never cleared as a benign faint.

red_flags.description: Unheralded syncope at rest with no prodrome, in a young otherwise-healthy adult
red_flags.clinical_significance: Syncope at rest without warning strongly suggests an arrhythmic (rather than reflex) mechanism and, with the family history, an inherited channelopathy at risk of sudden death.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Labelled a simple faint; the patient suffers a recurrent fatal ventricular arrhythmia without monitoring or defibrillation available.

red_flags.description: Brief violent palpitation immediately preceding the loss of consciousness
red_flags.clinical_significance: A palpitation immediately before collapse points to a tachyarrhythmia (e.g. polymorphic VT) as the cause of the syncope.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The arrhythmic mechanism is missed and the event is misattributed to a reflex faint.

red_flags.description: Family history of young sudden cardiac death (brother's cardiac arrest at 28)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Young sudden cardiac death in a first-degree relative markedly raises the probability of an inherited channelopathy and the patient's own risk of sudden death.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to elicit/weight this leads to under-triage of a potentially fatal inherited condition.

red_flags.description: Early-onset atrial fibrillation in a young relative (mother diagnosed in her 30s)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Early-onset atrial fibrillation co-occurring with young sudden death in the family is a recognised pointer toward Short QT syndrome specifically.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing this specific familial pattern loses an important clue to the underlying channelopathy.

red_flags.description: Abnormally short corrected QT interval (QTc, e.g. <340 ms) on 12-lead ECG with a structurally normal heart
red_flags.clinical_significance: An abnormally short QTc on ECG is the diagnostic hallmark of Short QT syndrome; it requires a 12-lead ECG and cannot be obtained over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - a 12-lead ECG is required; the correct action is immediate emergency assessment with continuous monitoring.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: An unheralded collapse at rest with no prodrome, preceded by a palpitation and set against a malignant family history, is not vasovagal and must not be dismissed.
Loss of consciousness can be mistaken for a seizure; rapid recovery without tongue-biting, incontinence or a post-ictal state points to an arrhythmic cause needing cardiac assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Reassurance from a normal resting exam
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Short QT syndrome is an electrical disorder with a structurally normal heart; a normal examination does not exclude it - a 12-lead ECG measuring the QTc is required.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on the more familiar Long QT syndrome
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The malignant channelopathy here is characterised by an abnormally SHORT QTc; anchoring on prolonged-QT patterns/drug causes can misdirect the workup - the ECG must be measured and interpreted for a short QTc, not assumed.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to act on the six-month palpitation history
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Prior unprovoked palpitations are an under-weighted warning of a paroxysmal arrhythmia and reinforce urgency rather than reassurance.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG to measure the corrected QT interval (QTc) - specifically to identify an abnormally short QTc
Continuous cardiac monitoring to detect polymorphic VT/VF or paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
Cardiovascular examination for murmurs, clicks or a right ventricular heave and assessment of peripheral pulses
Echocardiography to confirm a structurally normal heart
Genetic testing and specialist electrophysiology assessment (including ICD consideration)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate ambulance (000) transfer to the emergency department for a 12-lead ECG and continuous cardiac monitoring; in the meantime keep the patient resting quietly and lying flat, avoid sudden movements and stimulants, and have the sibling stay with them and be ready to call 000 and start CPR if they collapse again and are unresponsive.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient that this was just a faint, or advising a next-day GP visit without an ECG/monitoring, risks a fatal recurrent ventricular arrhythmia.
